title: The hospital that replaced 30 radiologists with AI — 1 year later
post:
A year ago, a hospital network I work with made a controversial decision: they replaced 30 radiologists with an AI system. The headlines were brutal. "AI takes doctors' jobs." "Healthcare goes too far." I was skeptical myself.

Here's what actually happened: the AI system handled initial screenings and routine cases. The radiologists shifted to complex cases, consultations, and work that requires human judgment and patient interaction. Error rates dropped 40%. Turnaround time went from days to hours. Patient satisfaction improved because they got results faster.

Oh, and the radiologists? They're still employed. They just do more interesting work now. The narrative that this was about replacing humans was wrong. It was about augmenting them.

Are healthcare institutions moving fast enough on AI adoption, or too fast? Thoughts?
